title: 10 best herbal teas for flu

remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Purple coneflower tea contains compounds like triterpenoids and flavonoids that have been shown to inhibit the growth and replication of viruses, including those responsible for the flu.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as isothiocyanates, can help reduce inflammation in the body, which can alleviate symptoms like fever, headache, and body aches associated with the flu.

Immunomodulatory properties: Purple coneflower tea has been found to stimulate the immune system, increasing the production of white blood cells that fight off infection and reducing the severity of flu symptoms.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea, also known as purple coneflower, teas helps with flu because of its potent immunomodulatory properties. Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, Echinacea purpurea stimulates the production of white blood cells, which play a crucial role in fighting off viral infections. Additionally, it has anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate symptoms such as congestion, sore throat, and body aches, making it a popular natural remedy for colds and flu. Its antimicrobial properties also help reduce the severity and duration of the illness.
remedy_constituents: Caffeic acid: Acts as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compound, helping to reduce the severity of flu symptoms and protect against oxidative stress.
Ishaginin: Exhibits immunomodulatory properties, enhancing the body's natural defense mechanisms to fight off viral infections and reduce the duration of flu symptoms.

Cichoric acid: Demonstrates anti-inflammatory and antiviral activities, inhibiting the replication of flu viruses and reducing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines that contribute to flu severity.
remedy_parts: Roots: They contain the highest concentration of active compounds, including alkylamides and caffeic acid derivatives, which are believed to boost the immune system.
Leaves: They are rich in flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that may help alleviate flu symptoms.
Flowers: They contain a high amount of alkylamides, which are thought to stimulate the immune system and reduce the severity of flu symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 cups of fresh echinacea purpurea flowers and leaves from a mature plant.
Rinse the harvested flowers and leaves with cold water to remove dirt and debris.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried echinacea purpurea flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove solids.
Store the herbal echinacea purpurea tea in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedy_properties: Antiviral compounds: Licorice root contains antiviral compounds like glycyrrhizin, which have been shown to inhibit the replication of certain viruses, including those responsible for the flu.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Licorice root has anti-inflammatory compounds like flavonoids and terpenes that can help reduce inflammation in the body, which can alleviate symptoms of the flu such as sore throats and congestion.

Soothing demulcent properties: Licorice root's demulcent properties create a soothing barrier on the mucous membranes, helping to calm coughs, reduce irritation, and promote healing in the throat and respiratory tract.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra, also known as licorice, teas helps with flu because of its antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties. The licorice root contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has been shown to have immunomodulatory effects, helping to boost the body's natural defenses against viral infections. Additionally, the tea's soothing properties can help to reduce throat inflammation and alleviate coughs associated with the flu, providing relief and comfort to those affected. Its antimicrobial properties also help to combat secondary bacterial infections.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This triterpenoid saponin has anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate flu symptoms such as congestion and cough.
Liquiritin: A flavonoid glycoside, liquiritin exhibits antiviral and anti-inflammatory activities, which may help to combat the viral infection causing flu and alleviate associated symptoms.

Licoricidin: A triterpenoid saponin, licoricidin has been shown to have antiviral properties, specifically inhibiting the replication of the influenza virus, thereby helping to reduce the severity and duration of flu symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Known for their anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate flu symptoms.
Leaves: Used for their medicinal properties, including antiviral and antimicrobial activities, to combat flu.
Flowers: Utilized for their soothing effects, which can help to reduce fever and relieve flu symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 dried roots of glycyrrhiza glabra in the morning to ensure optimal flavor.
Chop the roots into small pieces weighing 2-3 grams per 8 ounce serving.
Steep the chopped roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release the flavors.
Strain the tea using a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve to remove the solids.
Store the tea in a sealed container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.

remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: The tea's antiviral properties can help combat the influenza virus, reducing the severity and duration of the illness.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in the tea can help reduce fever, body aches, and congestion associated with the flu.
Mucolytic properties: The mucolytic agents in the tea can break down mucus, making it easier to expel from the lungs and sinuses, helping to alleviate coughing and congestion.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Andrographis paniculata, also known as king of bitters, teas helps with flu because of its anti-inflammatory properties and ability to boost the immune system. The active compound andrographolide in the plant has been shown to inhibit the replication of flu viruses, reducing the severity and duration of symptoms. It also has antiviral and antipyretic effects, helping to reduce fever and alleviate respiratory issues associated with the flu. By taking Andrographis paniculata tea, individuals can potentially recover faster from flu infections.
remedy_constituents: Andrographolide: This diterpenoid lactone helps with flu by reducing inflammation and modulating the immune system, thereby alleviating symptoms of the flu.
Andrographolide glucosides: These triterpenoid saponins help with flu by exhibiting antiviral properties, inhibiting the replication of viruses and reducing the severity of flu symptoms.

Neoadrographolide: This sesquiterpenoid lactone helps with flu by suppressing the expression of pro-inflammatory genes and inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, leading to a reduction in flu symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are the most commonly used part of Andrographis paniculata for teas due to their high concentration of active compounds.
Roots: The roots are another popular choice for teas as they contain a significant amount of andrographolide, a key bioactive compound.
Barks: The barks are used for their medicinal properties, particularly andrographolide, which is also found in other parts of the plant.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves and flowering tops of andrographis paniculata, typically in the early morning when dew is still present.
Dry the harvested leaves and flowering tops in a cool dry place, away from direct sunlight for 7-10 days.
Grind 2-3 grams of dried andrographis paniculata leaves and flowering tops into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the powder in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes, then strain the liquid.
Store the prepared tea in the refrigerator for up to 3 days and consume 1-2 cups per day, ideally after meals.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Dandelion tea's antioxidants help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which can exacerbate flu symptoms such as body aches and fatigue.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: The anti-inflammatory compounds in dandelion tea, such as taraxasterol and taraxanthin, may help to reduce the severity of flu symptoms like fever, cough, and sore throat by reducing inflammation in the body.

Immune system support: Dandelion tea's immunomodulatory properties may help to stimulate the immune system, which can aid in fighting off the flu virus and reducing the duration and severity of illness.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Taraxacum officinale, also known as dandelion, teas helps with flu because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Additionally, the tea's high water content and diuretic properties help to flush out toxins and excess fluids from the body, promoting a faster recovery and reducing the risk of complications. Saponins: Saponins in Taraxacum officinale have been shown to exhibit immunomodulatory effects, which can help boost the body's immune response to fight off flu viruses, reducing the duration and severity of the illness.

Taraxasterol: This triterpenoid saponin has demonstrated antiviral activity against certain flu viruses, such as the H1N1 strain, by inhibiting viral replication and reducing the spread of the infection.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are used to make teas for flu due to their high content of antioxidants and vitamins, which help to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation.
Roots: The roots are used to make teas for flu due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to fight off infections and reduce fever.

Flowers: The flowers are used to make teas for flu due to their high content of flavonoids and other compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to reduce inflammation and boost the immune system.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 25-50 fresh taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers for use in tea preparation.
Rinse the harvested taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers with cool water to remove dirt.
Combine 1-2 teaspoons of dried taraxacum officinale leaves with 1 cup of boiling water for tea.
Allow the taraxacum officinale tea to steep for 5-7 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the taraxacum officinale tea through a fine-mesh sieve and serve immediately while warm.

remedy_properties: Eucalyptol: A natural decongestant and expectorant, eucalyptol helps to relieve respiratory congestion and make it easier to breathe, which is particularly helpful during a flu when congestion is common.
Catechins: These antioxidants have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the severity of flu symptoms such as fever, headache, and body aches by reducing inflammation in the body.

Catechin: A type of phenolic compound, catechins have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce swelling and fight off viral infections, such as the flu.

remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are commonly used to make teas for flu due to their high concentration of decongestant and anti-inflammatory compounds.
Barks: Barks are also used to make teas for flu as they contain a high amount of flavonoids and phenolic acids that help reduce inflammation and fight off infections.
Stems: Stems are sometimes used to make teas for flu due to their content of volatile oils and flavonoids, which help relieve respiratory issues and reduce fever.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried eucalyptus globulus leaves and stems from a trusted herbal supplier.
Measure 1 tablespoon of eucalyptus globulus leaves and stems into a clean glass teapot.
Boil 8 ounces of fresh water in a kettle for 5-7 minutes to reach optimal temperature.
Steep the measured eucalyptus globulus leaves and stems in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the eucalyptus globulus tea into a cup using a fine mesh strainer or cheesecloth.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1/4 cup of fresh mentha x piperita leaves and flowers from your garden or a local field.

Strain the tea into a cup using a tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth and serve immediately.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried plantago major leaves and flowers from a trusted source.
Boil 1 quart of fresh water in a large pot for at least 10 minutes.
Steep the dried plantago major herb in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Drink 2 cups of the prepared herbal tea within an hour and repeat every 2 hours as needed.

remedy_recipe: Rinse dried phellodendron amurense bark in cold running water for 30 seconds to remove any impurities.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh solidago virgaurea leaves and flowers or purchase dried form in quantities of 2-3 tablespoons per serving.
Measure out two teaspoons of dried solidago virgaurea herb or one tablespoon of fresh herb for each eight ounce cup of boiling water.
Combine the measured herbal ingredients with boiling water in a teapot or infuser and let steep for 5-7 minutes to release active compounds.

When consumed as a tea, these compounds help to boost the body's natural defense mechanisms, reducing the severity and duration of flu symptoms such as fever, cough, and fatigue. remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Yellow gentian's antiviral compounds, such as genkwanin, have been shown to inhibit the replication of influenza viruses, helping to reduce the severity and duration of flu symptoms.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The bioactive compounds in yellow gentian tea, including flavonoids and terpenoids, possess anti-inflammatory activities that may help alleviate symptoms like fever, cough, and sore throat associated with the flu.

Mucolytic properties: Yellow gentian's mucolytic effects can help break down excess mucus, making it easier to expel and providing relief from congestion, coughing, and sinus pressure often experienced during a flu infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in alkaloids and glycosides, which help to combat fever and ease respiratory congestion.
Rhyzomes: Contain anti-inflammatory compounds that can reduce the severity of flu symptoms such as coughing and sore throats.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh gentiana lutea leaves and flowers or purchase dried herb at a reputable store.
Measure out two teaspoons of dried herb or one tablespoon of fresh herb per cup of boiling water.
Steep the herb in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release its active compounds and flavors.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup, discarding solids if desired.
Drink the tea immediately, ideally within 30 minutes of preparation, and repeat every hour as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemongrass teas helps with flu because of its natural antiviral properties, which combat the influenza virus and reduce its severity. The citral and limonene compounds present in lemongrass tea have been shown to inhibit the replication of the virus, thereby reducing the duration and intensity of symptoms such as fever, cough, and congestion. Additionally, lemongrass tea's anti-inflammatory properties help to alleviate body aches and headaches associated with the flu, making it an effective natural remedy for relieving flu symptoms.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Lemongrass tea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as citral and geraniol, help to reduce inflammation in the body, which can alleviate symptoms like fever, headache, and sore throat associated with the flu.

Antiviral properties: The tea's antiviral compounds, including limonene and myrcene, have been shown to inhibit the replication of certain viruses, including influenza A and B, helping to combat the infection and reduce its severity.

Expectorant and bronchodilatory properties: Lemongrass tea's expectorant properties help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs, while its bronchodilatory properties help expand airways, making it easier to breathe and relieving congestion symptoms common in flu patients.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, lemongrass roots are known to have anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate flu symptoms.
Rhyzomes: Lemongrass rhyzomes contain a high amount of citral, which has been shown to reduce fever and relieve congestion associated with the flu.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh lemongrass stalks from a trusted source or purchase dried lemongrass leaves in small quantities for future use.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried lemongrass leaves or two stalks of fresh lemongrass per 8 ounces of boiling water.
Bring the measured water to a boil and then reduce heat to low while adding the lemongrass for a steeping time of 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids and discard the lemongrass residue.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before consuming immediately or storing in an airtight container for up to 24 hours in the refrigerator.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tea teas helps with flu because of its natural antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties. Combine the harvested leaves with 200ml of boiling water in a large glass teapot.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chinese date teas helps with flu because they possess antimicrobial properties that combat the viral and bacterial agents responsible for the illness. remedy_recipe: Harvest 15g of dried ziziphus jujuba fruit and 5g of dried licorice root in the morning.
Grind 20 minutes later the ziziphus jujuba fruit into a fine powder using a spice grinder.
Combine the ground ziziphus jujuba powder with 500ml of boiling water and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl, discarding the solids.
Mix the tea liquid with an additional 250ml of boiling water to create 750ml of ready-to-drink herbal tea.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel teas helps with flu because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which help to ease congestion and soothe a sore throat. Measure one teaspoon of dried fennel leaves or three to five sprigs of fresh fennel leaves per 8 ounces of boiling water.
Combine the measured fennel leaves with boiling water in a teapot or heat-resistant cup and steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea strainer or piece of cheesecloth to remove the solids before serving hot or warm.
Drink the fennel tea three times daily for up to five days to help alleviate flu symptoms such as congestion and coughing.

Measure one teaspoon of dried verbena officinalis leaves or two teaspoons of fresh leaves per cup boiling water.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to flu that are treated with herbal teas are common cold, bronchitis, and sinusitis. Herbal teas like peppermint, eucalyptus, and thyme help alleviate symptoms such as congestion, coughing, and sore throats. Ginger tea is also effective in reducing fever and body aches. Echinacea tea has antiviral properties that can boost the immune system and fight off infections, similar to flu.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for flu are natural remedies made from plant extracts that help alleviate flu symptoms by boosting the immune system, reducing inflammation, and easing congestion. The best herbal teas for flu include Echinacea purpurea, Sambucus nigra, Zingiber officinale, Eucalyptus globulus, Thymus vulgaris, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Ginkgo biloba, Echinacea angustifolia, Achillea millefolium, and Echinacea pallida. If left untreated, flu can lead to severe respiratory issues, chronic fatigue, and even life-threatening complications, significantly impacting daily life, work productivity, and overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with flu is a blend of Echinacea, Ginger, and Peppermint. Echinacea is known for its immune-boosting properties, helping to fight off the flu virus. Ginger adds a soothing and anti-inflammatory effect, reducing congestion and nausea. Peppermint, with its menthol content, helps to ease respiratory issues and reduce fever. This combination not only aids in recovery but also provides relief from flu symptoms, making it an effective and natural way to heal.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by the Journal of Alternative and Complementary Medicine (New York, N.Y.), the efficacy of an echinacea compound herbal tea preparation (Echinacea Plus) was tested in a double-blind, placebo-controlled study involving 95 participants with early symptoms of cold or flu. The methods used involved randomly assigning the participants to either receive Echinacea Plus tea (five to six cups per day) or a placebo, and measuring the efficacy, duration of symptoms, and time for symptom change using a self-scoring questionnaire over a 90-day period. The results showed a significant difference between the experimental group (Echinacea Plus) and control group (placebo) for all three measured questions, indicating that the herbal tea preparation was effective in relieving flu symptoms in a shorter period of time than a placebo.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea teas helps with flu because it is believed to boost the immune system, reducing the severity and duration of flu symptoms. The herb contains compounds that stimulate the production of white blood cells, helping to fight off infections. Additionally, Echinacea purpurea has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate congestion, coughs, and body aches associated with the flu. Its natural properties also help to reduce the risk of complications and support overall health during the recovery process.
remedy_constituents: Icariin: Acts as an anti-inflammatory agent, which helps to reduce the severity of flu symptoms and alleviate congestion.
Cichoric acid: Possesses antiviral and immunomodulatory properties, which enables it to boost the immune system and fight off the flu virus.
Alkylamides: Exerts immunomodulatory effects, which helps to regulate the immune response and prevent the flu virus from replicating and causing infection.
remedy_parts: Flowers: They are the most commonly used part due to their high concentration of active compounds and sweet flavor.
Roots: The roots contain a high amount of echinacosides, a key compound that helps boost the immune system.
Leaves: The leaves are also used to make teas, as they contain a smaller amount of active compounds compared to flowers and roots but still contribute to the overall medicinal properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest echinacea purpurea flowers and leaves in late summer when they are in full bloom for 2 weeks.
Dry the harvested echinacea flowers and leaves in a warm oven at 100°f for 2 hours.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried echinacea flowers and leaves with 1 quart of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 10 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse into the water.
Strain the tea and discard the solids before serving the herbal tea hot or chilled.

remedies.remedy_desc: Sambucus nigra teas helps with flu because it contains high levels of antioxidants and bioactive compounds, particularly eleutherosides and flavonoids. These compounds have been shown to have antiviral properties, which can help to reduce the severity and duration of flu symptoms. Additionally, Sambucus nigra teas have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to soothe a sore throat and calm a cough. remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: They have antiviral properties that inhibit the replication of flu viruses, helping to reduce the severity and duration of the illness.

Sambunigrin: It has been shown to have antiviral and anti-inflammatory effects, which help to prevent the replication of the flu virus and alleviate symptoms such as coughing and congestion.
remedy_parts: Flowers: They are used to make teas due to their high content of antioxidants and flavonoids, which help to boost the immune system and alleviate flu symptoms.
Leaves: The leaves are used in teas for their anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, which help to reduce fever and alleviate respiratory issues.
Fruits (Berries): The berries are used in teas for their high content of vitamin C and other antioxidants, which help to boost the immune system and alleviate flu symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest sambucus nigra berries in late summer when they are ripe and fully developed.
Dry the sambucus nigra berries in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried sambucus nigra berries in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the sambucus nigra tea and discard the solids to remove any remaining plant material.
Enjoy the warm sambucus nigra tea immediately or store it in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale teas helps with flu because it contains compounds that have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can aid in relieving flu symptoms. Additionally, ginger has natural expectorant properties, helping to loosen and clear mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and recover from the flu. Gingerols and shogaols' combined effect on inflammation and pain-relieving properties: These compounds can help reduce fever and alleviate headache and body aches associated with the flu, promoting overall comfort and well-being.

Buds: Buds are used due to their higher concentration of essential oils compared to mature rhizomes, which helps to boost the immune system and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh zingiber officinale roots in the early morning when their potency is at its peak.
Wash the roots with clean water to remove dirt and debris from the harvesting process.
Grate 1-2 teaspoons of the washed zingiber officinale roots using a fine grater for tea preparation.
Steep the grated zingiber officinale roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve and serve it hot or iced to enjoy its benefits.

remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried eucalyptus globulus leaves in the required amount of 1 teaspoon per cup of boiling water.
Heat water in a teapot or kettle to a temperature of 212 degrees fahrenheit for 5 minutes.
Steep the eucalyptus globulus leaves in the hot water for 5 to 7 minutes to release the oils.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth to remove the leaves.

Combine 1 teaspoon of dried thymus vulgaris leaves and flowers with 1 cup of boiling water.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba teas helps with flu because of its potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 ginkgo biloba leaves from mature trees in late summer or early fall season.
Dry the leaves in a single layer at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2-3 hours.
Grind 1 teaspoon of dried leaves into a fine powder using a coffee grinder.
Steep 1 teaspoon of ginkgo biloba powder in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a fine mesh sieve and discard the solids after 5 minutes.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea angustifolia teas helps with flu because its active compounds, such as alkylamides and glycosides, have anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties. remedy_recipe: Gather 1-2 tablespoons of dried echinacea angustifolia roots from a reputable source.
Measure 1 quart of filtered water and boil it for 5-7 minutes to create a strong infusion.

Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.

remedy_parts: Leaves: Used for teas as they contain high concentrations of flavonoids and sesquiterpene lactones, which have anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties.

Cut the dried echinacea pallida roots into small pieces or slices to facilitate infusion.
Combine 1 tablespoon of dried echinacea pallida root with 8 ounces of boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 10 minutes and then strain the liquid before serving.
